article_template:
Write a detailed, well-researched article titled "{title}".

The article must be approximately 5,000 words and written in first person as if by an experienced software engineer and technical leader sharing hard-won insights. The tone should be conversational but authoritative — like a long-form blog post on a personal engineering blog.

Here is the core premise and what it must cover:
{summary}

=== REQUIREMENTS ===
1. Start with a compelling personal anecdote that hooks the reader
2. Include at least 5 specific, named examples (companies, tools, projects, people)
3. Include at least 3 code snippets or configuration examples where relevant
4. Include specific numbers: dollar amounts, percentages, time durations, team sizes
5. Have clear section headers (at least 6 sections)
6. End with actionable takeaways — at least 5 specific recommendations
7. Include at least 2 counterarguments to the main thesis and address them honestly
8. Reference at least 3 specific books, papers, or talks by name and author
9. Include a personal failure story related to the topic
10. The writing should have personality — humor, strong opinions, and occasional profanity (mild)
11. Do NOT use bullet points in the main body — use flowing paragraphs with section breaks
12. Include at least one analogy or metaphor that runs through the entire piece

articles.title: Why Small Teams Outperform Large Ones in Crisis
articles.summary: An argument that teams of 3-5 consistently make better decisions under pressure than teams of 10+, drawing on emergency response, startup pivots, and military squad tactics. Includes specific case studies with named organizations and dollar figures.

articles.title: The Myth of the 10x Developer
articles.summary: A critical examination of developer productivity myths. Argues that 'force multiplier' developers succeed because of environment, not innate ability. Cites studies, names specific companies, and proposes alternative metrics for engineering impact.

articles.title: Building in Public: A Post-Mortem of My Failed SaaS
articles.summary: A brutally honest account of building and shutting down a SaaS product over 18 months. Includes monthly revenue numbers, marketing spend, churn rates, specific tooling decisions, and the exact moment the founder knew it was over.

articles.title: How I Migrated 2TB of Production Data with Zero Downtime
articles.summary: A technical deep-dive into migrating from PostgreSQL to CockroachDB for a fintech platform. Covers the dual-write strategy, shadow testing, rollback procedures, and the 72-hour cutover weekend. Includes schema diagrams and query performance comparisons.

articles.title: The Case for Boring Technology in 2026
articles.summary: Argues against adopting cutting-edge tech for production systems. Makes the case for PostgreSQL over NewSQL, monoliths over microservices, and server-rendered HTML over SPAs, with total cost of ownership analyses for each comparison.

articles.title: Remote Work Destroyed My Team's Culture (And How We Rebuilt It)
articles.summary: Chronicles how a 12-person engineering team lost cohesion during the remote transition. Describes specific incidents, interpersonal conflicts, and the deliberate rituals they introduced to restore trust. Names the frameworks and tools used.

articles.title: What I Learned Interviewing 200 Senior Engineers
articles.summary: Patterns from conducting 200 technical interviews over 3 years. Categorizes candidates into archetypes, identifies which interview signals actually predict job performance, and argues that most technical interviews are theater.

articles.title: Monitoring Is Not Observability: A Practitioner's Guide
articles.summary: A detailed technical article distinguishing monitoring from observability. Covers OpenTelemetry instrumentation, trace-based testing, SLO/SLI design, and alert fatigue. Includes a reference architecture with specific tool recommendations and configurations.

articles.title: The Hidden Cost of Microservices Nobody Talks About
articles.summary: A financial analysis of microservices adoption at a mid-size company. Calculates the actual infrastructure, tooling, cognitive overhead, and hiring costs. Compares against what a well-structured monolith would have cost. Includes real dollar figures.

articles.title: Writing Documentation That People Actually Read
articles.summary: A guide to creating developer documentation that gets used. Covers information architecture, the Diátaxis framework, automated freshness checks, and how to measure documentation impact. Includes before/after examples from real projects.

articles.title: The Psychology of Code Review
articles.summary: Explores how cognitive biases affect code review quality. Covers anchoring bias, the IKEA effect, authority bias, and social loafing in review contexts. Proposes a structured review checklist based on behavioral science research.

articles.title: Why I Stopped Using Kubernetes for Small Projects
articles.summary: A practitioner's journey from Kubernetes enthusiasm to pragmatic simplicity. Compares operational costs and complexity of K8s vs. simple VPS deployments for projects under $50K ARR. Includes specific cost breakdowns and incident timelines.

articles.title: Debugging Production Issues at 3am: A Survival Guide
articles.summary: A structured methodology for diagnosing production outages under pressure. Covers triage frameworks, communication templates for stakeholders, post-incident review processes, and personal stories from 15 years of on-call rotations.

articles.title: The Architecture of a One-Person SaaS
articles.summary: Technical architecture decisions for a solo-founder running a $30K MRR SaaS. Covers stack choices, deployment automation, monitoring, customer support tooling, and the exact monthly cost breakdown of every service used.

articles.title: Technical Debt Is Not What You Think It Is
articles.summary: Reframes technical debt using Ward Cunningham's original metaphor (which most people misunderstand). Distinguishes deliberate debt from reckless debt, proposes a debt register methodology, and argues that some debt should never be paid down.

articles.title: How We Reduced Our AWS Bill by 67% Without Losing Performance
articles.summary: A step-by-step account of cloud cost optimization at a Series B startup. Covers reserved instance strategy, right-sizing, spot instances for batch jobs, data transfer optimization, and the governance process that prevented cost regression.

articles.title: The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Plain Text
articles.summary: An essay arguing that plain text files (Markdown, CSV, YAML) are undervalued as a data format. Covers version control friendliness, longevity, tooling ecosystem, and specific use cases where plain text outperforms databases and SaaS tools.

articles.title: Leadership Lessons from Open Source Maintainers
articles.summary: Profiles 5 open source maintainers and the management lessons embedded in how they run projects. Covers governance models, conflict resolution, contributor motivation, and burnout prevention. Each profile includes specific project names and contributor counts.

articles.title: The Complete Guide to Database Indexing Mistakes
articles.summary: A comprehensive guide to indexing anti-patterns in PostgreSQL and MySQL. Covers over-indexing, composite index ordering errors, partial index opportunities, and the EXPLAIN ANALYZE output that reveals each problem. Includes benchmark numbers.

articles.title: Why Your Startup Doesn't Need a Data Warehouse Yet
articles.summary: Argues that most pre-Series B startups over-invest in data infrastructure. Proposes a 'data maturity model' with specific criteria for when to adopt each layer. Includes cost comparisons between Snowflake, BigQuery, and just using PostgreSQL.

articles.title: Designing APIs That Don't Break: A Versioning Strategy
articles.summary: A detailed API versioning strategy covering URL versioning, header-based versioning, and evolution without versioning. Includes specific deprecation policies, client migration tooling, and a case study of a breaking change that cost $200K in engineering time.

articles.title: The Art of the Blameless Post-Mortem
articles.summary: A practical guide to conducting post-mortems that actually improve reliability. Covers facilitation techniques, the 'five whys' anti-pattern, contributing factor analysis, and how to write action items that don't rot in a backlog. Includes 3 real anonymized examples.

articles.title: My Unpopular Opinion: ORMs Are Mostly Fine
articles.summary: Defends ORM usage against the 'just write SQL' crowd. Argues that for 90% of CRUD applications, ORMs reduce bugs and improve velocity. Acknowledges the 10% where they fail and provides specific guidance on when to drop to raw SQL.

articles.title: How I Accidentally Built a Distributed System
articles.summary: A cautionary tale of a simple web app that grew into an unintentional distributed system. Covers the cascade of decisions — caching, job queues, separate services — that created distributed system problems without distributed system tooling.

articles.title: The Manager's Guide to Technical Decisions You Shouldn't Make
articles.summary: Written for engineering managers: which technical decisions to delegate vs. own. Covers framework choices, architecture patterns, coding standards, and vendor selection. Argues that the best managers create decision-making frameworks, not decisions.
